summary.catchwords: FAMILY LAW – PARENTING – SAFETY – Where the Court has previously found that the applicant’s husband engaged in the grooming of a young person – Where the Court previously restrained the applicant from causing the children to come into contact with her husband – Where the applicant seeks to remove this prior restraint – Where the applicant seeks orders to reintegrate her husband into the children’s lives –Where the applicant’s application is opposed by the respondent –Where the Independent Children’s Lawyer supports the applicant’s proposal – Where the Court finds that the applicant’s husband remains an unacceptable risk to the children – Where the Court dismisses the applicant’s application. FAMILY LAW – PARENTING – SPEND TIME – Where the Court previously made orders mandating that the respondent spend only supervised time with the children – Where the respondent seeks orders to remove the requirement for professional supervision– Where the respondent seeks orders to extend his time with the children – Where the applicant and Independent Children’s Lawyer oppose the orders sought by the respondent – Where the Independent Children’s Lawyer contends that the respondent’s supervised time with the children ought to be extended by three hours – Where the Court finds that no change to the previous orders are warranted– Where the Court dismisses the respondent’s proposal. FAMILY LAW – COSTS – Where the applicant agreed to pay half of the costs of the Independent Children’s Lawyer – Where the respondent refused to pay a half share of the Independent Children’s Lawyer’s costs –Where the respondent contends he is unable to afford his share of the cost of the Independent Children’s Lawyer – Where the applicant is silent on this issue – Where the Court does not accept the respondent’s contention – Where the Court orders that the respondent pay half of the costs of the Independent Children’s Lawyer.

fulltext:
REASONS FOR JUDGMENT

ALTOBELLI J:

INTRODUCTION

These reasons for judgment explain the orders the Court has made in a dispute between the Applicant Mother (“the mother”) and the Respondent Father (“the father”) (collectively “the parents”) in relation to any potential change to the Final Orders made by this Court on 31 March 2022 (“Final Orders”) which govern the parenting arrangements for their two children, X born in 2014 and Y born in 2016 (collectively “the children”).
On 31 March 2022, after a four-day hearing in November 2021, I made orders for the mother to have sole parental responsibility of the children, for the children to live with the mother, and for the father to have professionally supervised time with the children each alternate Saturday or Sunday (“the Final Orders”). I also made protective orders restraining the mother from bringing the children into contact with Mr M, her now husband (Final Orders, Order 8). In my reasons for judgement explaining those orders, on no less than four occasions, I referred to the mother’s “blinkered view” about the risks posed to the children by Mr M (Fontain & Pretre (No 2) [2022] FedCFamC1F 200 (“Fontain & Pretre (No 2)”) at [54]–[55] and [118]–[119]). Indeed, in Fontain & Pretre (No 2) at [118] I said:
He presents a very real risk to the children, and the mother’s blinkered view about him, and his grooming activities, represent a major deficit in her parenting capacity.
In closing submissions in the present case, the mother’s senior counsel submitted that, “the blinkers are well and truly off” (Transcript 4 September 2025, p. 30 line 28) and that, in effect, there was no longer any risk to the safety of the children arising from Mr M.
As it turns out, the evidence demonstrates, and the Court finds, that the mother’s blinkers are not well and truly off. Mr M constitutes an unacceptable risk to the safety of the children. In Fontain & Pretre (No 2) at [34]–[36], the Court found that Mr M had engaged in grooming behaviour with a young person (“the grooming incident”). The Court noted at [35] of the same that “Some of the messages [between Mr M and the young person] are chilling to read”. The Court maintains this view. Order 8 of the Final Orders was made to mitigate the risk posed to the children by Mr M. Moreover, the Court finds that during the same period that Mr M was engaged in grooming a 13-year-old young person (“young person”), the mother was introducing her children to him, and they were spending time together. Indeed, she told Dr CC that Mr M was a “father figure” for the children (Expert Report of Dr CC dated 4 October 2023, paragraph 45). On 4 November 2023, the mother and Mr M married.

BACKGROUND

The mother is presently 32 years old and is a stay-at-home mother. The father is 47 years old and works in property maintenance for his family business. An appropriate background of this matter is set out in Fontain & Pretre (No 2) at [2]–[14], reproduced below:
...The parents commenced cohabitation in 2012 when the mother was 19 years old. The mother asserts that she commenced an intimate relationship with the father in 2008, when she was 15 years old ... The father contends their intimate relationship commenced when the mother was 18 years old. Irrespective of which age it was, there is a significant age difference between the parents.

The relationship was tumultuous. Each parent makes very serious allegations against the other, all of which are denied. The allegations include family violence including coercive and controlling violence, neglect and abuse of the children, mental health difficulties, and abuse of drugs and alcohol. By the time of the final hearing, however, the only relevant allegations related to family violence, including coercive and controlling violence. As will become evident, there were also concerns about the parenting capacity of both of the parents.

Shortly after the parents separated, with the father leaving the matrimonial home located in [Suburb O], New South Wales (“the matrimonial home”), an informal agreement was reached between the parents whereby the father would spend time with the children each Tuesday and Saturday from 9.00am to 5.00pm. In August 2018 the mother and the children moved from the matrimonial home into the maternal grandparent’s home in [Suburb C], New South Wales. The father subsequently moved back into the matrimonial home. Between 10 August and 22 August 2018 the father retained the children contrary to the parents’ agreement. The father contends that his reasoning was that he had concerns about the children’s welfare in the mother’s care. The mother commenced the present proceedings in August 2018 and on 22 August 2018 Baumann J made orders for the father to return the children to the mother by 5.00pm that day. On 24 August 2018 Baumann J made orders that the children were to live with the father each Tuesday from 9.00am to 5.00pm, each alternate weekend from Friday 5.00pm until Monday 9.00am, and each alternate Thursday from after day care to Friday 5.00pm, with the children to live with their mother at all other times.

The mother subsequently filed an Application in a Case seeking to discharge the orders made by Baumann J, and for the children to live with her and to spend supervised time with the father. On 27 August 2018 McClelland J (as his Honour then was) suspended the orders made 24 August 2018, and ordered that the children live with the mother, spend supervised time with the father and appointed an Independent Children’s Lawyer.

On 24 September 2018 Johnston J in the Family Court of Australia (as it was then known) varied the orders in relation to the father’s supervised time and made a further order that the mother was to ensure that the maternal uncle not care for the children, or be present with the children without her likewise being present. No party to the present proceedings has sought a variation to that order.

In December 2018 the children were assessed by a paediatrician, and [Y] was diagnosed as having expressive and receptive language delay, a degree of global developmental delay, and autistic features that warranted further assessment by a developmental paediatrician.

Late in 2018, or early 2019 the mother commenced a relationship with her present partner, [Mr M]. The Court will find that, based on the evidence before it, [Mr M] presents a risk of harm to the children because of his online grooming activities of a young person.

Between 29 January 2019 and 9 July 2019 the mother asserts that the father did not spend time with the children.

On 7 February 2019 [Dr K], a developmental paediatrician, formally assessed [Y] and diagnosed him to have autism spectrum disorder level 3 and global developmental delay.

In July 2019 the father filed an Application in a Case seeking unsupervised time with the children. On 5 September 2019 Senior Registrar Campbell (as he then was) dismissed this application, but did make an order that the father spend not less than eight hours’ time weekly with the children supervised by a commercial agency as agreed, but failing agreement through [L Services]. Such supervision was to be at the father’s cost. On a without admissions basis, the mother was restrained from permitting the children to be in the unsupervised care of the maternal uncle, and in the unsupervised presence of [Mr M].

Between 31 December 2019 and 17 October 2020 the father did not spend time with the children.

On 22 December 2020 the father filed an urgent Application in a Case seeking to restrain [Mr M] from spending any time with the children. On 23 December 2020 I made that order.

In April 2021 the parents sensibly settled all matters outstanding in relation to alteration of property interests. In September 2021 the Family Report prepared by [Ms P] was prepared and subsequently released. The matter was heard by me between 1 and 4 November 2021. The children had the benefit of an experienced Independent Children’s Lawyer, with an equally experienced counsel appearing on behalf of the Independent Children’s Lawyer. Both parents were represented by experienced family lawyers and family law counsel, including senior counsel for the mother.

PROCEDURAL HISTORY

The parents have had a substantial litigation history before this Court. The first tranche of proceedings involving the parents commenced in August 2018 and concluded in March 2022 when the Final Orders were made. The parents returned to the Court on 24 October 2023 when the mother filed an Initiating Application that sought to discharge Order 8 of the Final Orders; the restraint imposed on the mother from bringing the children into contact with Mr M. Twenty-five orders have been made during the course of these subsequent proceedings since December 2023. By virtue of the length and complexity of these proceedings, these reasons for judgment will only traverse materially relevant orders and determinations.
On 5 February 2024, Senior Judicial Registrar Buttriss transferred the matter to Division 1 of this Court and appointed an Independent Children’s Lawyer.
On 20 March 2024, I listed the matter for determination of the parents’ applications to re-open the proceedings pursuant to s 65DAAA of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) (“the Act”) on 15 May 2024.
On 2 July 2024, orders were made with the consent of the parties to re-open the proceedings and pursue their respective applications. A Single Expert Report of Dr U (“the Single Joint Expert”) was also ordered.
On 9 September 2024, leave was granted to file and serve an updated affidavit of Dr CC annexing her report dated 25 July 2024, and an order pursuant to s 102NA of the Act was made.
On 20 February 2025, I reserved judgment in respect of the parents’ interim applications, and granted leave for Mr M to provide a copy of the Single Joint Expert Report dated 13 November 2024 (“the Single Joint Expert Report”) to his respective treaters.
On 4 April 2025, I made a series of orders setting the matter down for Final Hearing with an estimated hearing time of four days. I further delivered an interim judgment which dismissed the mother’s Application in a Proceeding filed 18 December 2024, the father’s subsequent Response to Application in a Proceeding filed 30 January 2025 and I restrained the father by way of injunction, on a without admissions basis, from physically approaching or observing the mother. Costs of the interim proceedings were reserved to be considered during the final hearing.
On 19 August 2025, in my absence, Campton J presided over an Application in a Proceeding filed 15 August 2025 by the mother to vacate the final hearing dates. His Honour dismissed the mother’s Application in a Proceeding and extended the time for compliance with the trial filing deadlines made on 4 April 2025.
On 1 September 2025; the first day of the final hearing, I ordered that the father provide a precise Minute of Order sought prior to 10:00 am the following day.
On 4 September 2025, I reserved my judgment in relation to the final hearing and ordered that the mother pay one half of the Independent Children’s Lawyer’s costs for the same.
On 17 September 2025, my Chambers contacted the parties in relation to an oversight by the parties whereby they did not seek to formally tender the exhibits to the Affidavit of Dr CC filed 25 August 2025 (“Affidavit of Dr CC”). The Court, on its own motion sought to tender these annexures. All parties were agreeable to this course, and the annexures to the Affidavit of Dr CC became Exhibit C1.
On 19 September 2025, my Chambers contacted the parties in relation to Annexure C of the Affidavit of Dr CC, which was her Report dated 4 October 2023 (“Dr CC’s Report dated 4 October 2023”). Paragraph 91 of Dr CC’s Report dated 4 October 2023 refers to a text message exchange between the young person’s father and Mr M which was not in evidence. The Court sought whether any of the parties would object to the text message exchange being tendered into evidence.
On 24 September 2025, the Court received a response on behalf of the mother which consented to the text message exchange being tendered and on the provision that the Court considered further brief submissions in relation to the text messages. On 25 September 2025, the Court granted leave for the mother to rely on her brief submissions in relation to this issue and invited the father and Independent Children’s Lawyer to similarly make some short submissions.
On 29 September 2025, the father provided his brief written submissions in relation to the text message exchange between the young person’s father and Mr M. The Court did not receive a response from the Independent Children’s Lawyer in relation to this subject, and subsequently, the matter was re-listed before the Court on 16 October 2025.
On 16 October 2025, the Court heard from all parties in relation to the text message exchange between the young person’s father and Mr M, and Exhibit ICL1 was amended to reflect the full text message exchange. Upon settling this discrepancy in the evidence, judgment was reserved.

EVIDENCE BEFORE THE COURT

In support of her case, the mother relied on the following documents:
(1) Case Summary Document filed 27 August 2025;

(2) Amended Initiating Application filed 25 August 2025;

(3) Notice of Child Abuse, Family Violence or Risk filed 13 December 2023;

(4) Her Affidavit filed 25 August 2025;

(5) Affidavit of Mr M filed 25 August 2025;

(6) Affidavit of Ms EE filed 25 August 2025;

(7) Affidavit of Ms FF filed 25 August 2025;

(8) Affidavit of Mr GG filed 25 August 2025;

(9) Affidavit of Mr JJ filed 25 August 2025;

(10) Affidavit of Ms KK filed 25 August 2025;

(11) Affidavit of Ms HH filed 25 August 2025;

(12) Affidavit and Annexures of Dr CC filed 25 August 2025;

(13) Email from the mother’s legal representative sent to Chambers on 24 September 2025 at 1:00 pm containing submissions in relation to the text message exchange referred to at Annexure C of the Affidavit of Dr CC (Exhibit C2); and

(14) Various documents tendered throughout the hearing which became Exhibits A1-A9.

In support of his case, the father relied on the following documents:
(1) Outline of Case Document (Interim Hearing) filed 17 February 2025;

(2) Amended Response to Final Orders filed 30 January 2025;

(3) The father’s Minute of Order handed up on 2 September 2025 (Exhibit R1);

(4) His Affidavit filed 21 November 2024;

(5) His Affidavit filed 30 January 2025;

(6) His Affidavit filed 25 August 2025;

(7) Email from the father sent to Chambers on 29 September 2025 at 2:36 pm containing submissions in relation to the text message exchange referred to at Annexure C of the Affidavit of Dr CC (Exhibit C3); and

(8) Various documents tendered throughout the hearing which became Exhibits R1-R11.

In support of her case, the Independent Children’s Lawyer relied on the following documents:
(1) Case Summary Document filed 1 September 2025;

(2) Orders made 31 March 2022;

(3) Fontain & Pretre (No 2) [2022] FedCFamC1F 200;

(4) Family Report of Dr U dated 13 November 2024;

(5) Costs Notice filed 29 August 2025; and

(6) Various documents tendered throughout the hearing which became Exhibits ICL1-ICL8.

During the trial, the Court heard from several witnesses. In the mother’s case, I heard from her, Mr M, Dr CC, Mr JJ, Mr GG, and Ms FF. In the father’s case I heard from him only. All parties also had the opportunity to cross-examine the Single Joint Expert. The Court notes that despite calling Mr JJ, Mr GG, and Ms FF as witnesses, the mother did not seek to tender the annexures to their respective affidavits which contain their reports. In a similar vein, despite seeking to rely on the affidavits of Ms EE, Ms KK and Ms HH the mother did not tender the annexures to their respective affidavits, nor did she call them as witnesses. Therefore, the relevant information in the affidavits of Mr JJ, Mr GG, Ms FF, Ms EE, Ms KK and Ms HH are not in evidence.
The Court refers the parties to r 8.15(3) of the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Family Law) Rules 2021 (Cth) (“the Rules”) where it sets out:
Requirements for affidavits
3) A document that is to be used in conjunction with an affidavit:
a) must be identified in the affidavit; and

b) must be filed as an annexure or an exhibit to the affidavit; and

c) must be paginated; and
d) must bear a statement signed by the person before whom the affidavit is made identifying it as the particular annexure or exhibit referred to in the affidavit; and

e) must not be accepted as evidence in the proceeding unless and until it is tendered in evidence at the hearing of the application and accepted into evidence by the court.

A document annexed or exhibited to an affidavit must be served with the affidavit.
The Court reminded the parties and practitioners of this on the first day of hearing by stating:
HIS HONOUR: ... you must tender documents separately. It can be at the end of the trial. You can do it now. It’s up to you. But the mere reference to a document in a tender bundle or as annexures to affidavits, means nothing. They must be tendered.

(Transcript 1 September 2025, p. 63 lines 10 – 15).
Indeed, the Court even reached out to the parties, as explained above, in relation to the annexures to Dr CC’s affidavit to confirm whether the mother intended to tender the same. Further, my Chambers supplied the parties and Independent Children’s Lawyer with a copy of the Exhibit list. No application, oral or otherwise, was made to include the annexures to any other treater’s affidavits. The Court must view the failure to admit certain evidence by the mother’s senior counsel and accredited family law specialist lawyers as an intentional choice.

COMPETING PROPOSALS

The mother

The mother’s proposed final orders are contained within her Amended Initiating Application filed 25 August 2025 (“the mother’s proposal”) comprising Annexure one of these reasons. The mother sought that the restraint against her from bringing the children into contact with Mr M be removed (Final Orders, Order 8), and the requirement for the mother to attend therapy be dispensed with (Final Orders, Orders 9–12). Further, the mother sought that the children commence spending supervised time with Mr M, firstly by a professional agency for a period of six months, and thereafter by her, with the children’s time to progress up to 24-hours per week with Mr M.
It was proposed that Mr M’s time with the children be conditional upon both Mr M’s and the mother’s continued attendance upon psychological therapy, an authority for Mr M’s psychologist to contact the mother should he cease therapy, and for Mr M’s psychologist to liaise with the Independent Children’s Lawyer and the mother’s psychologist. A complementary order was also sought that caused time between the children and Mr M to cease should any risk be identified by the private supervisor, the mother, the mother’s psychologist, or Mr M’s psychologist. The mother sought for electronic communication to occur between Mr M and the children after three-months of supervised time.
Lastly, the mother sought that the father be restrained from physically approaching or observing her and a final basis.

The father

The father provided the Court with his Final Proposed Minute of Order on 2 September 2025 (“the father’s proposal”), and this has been made Annexure two of these reasons. The father sought for the Final Orders relating to his time with the children be discharged (Final Orders, Orders 3–5). He also proposed that he commence spending unsupervised time with the children in a progressive regime which would culminate in the children spending ten–hours with him on Sunday and four–hours with him on Friday each week. The father proposed that changeover occur at the children’s school, and where this is not possible, that changeover be facilitated by an agreed professional supervision service, and failing agreement, with LL Service. Such cost of this supervision was to be shared equally between the parents.
Separately, the father proposed an order that he be required to engage in therapy sessions aimed towards improving his capacity to engage with the childrens needs. He further sought a protective order that, should the Court make findings that the mother poses a risk of harm to the children, that the children commence living with him on a full-time basis, and that the mother only have supervised time with the children
Finally, on a without admissions basis, the father proposed an order that he be restrained from approaching or following the mother.

The Independent Children’s Lawyer

The Independent Children’s Lawyer provided the Court with her Final Proposed Minute of Order on 3 September 2025 (“the Independent Children’s Lawyer’s proposal”), and this has been made Annexure three of these reasons. The Independent Children’s Lawyer sought that the mother have sole parental responsibility for long-term decision-making for the children and for the children to live with her. Further, the Independent Children’s Lawyer proposed that the children spend time with Mr M, including replica conditions of continued attendance upon therapy providers, and eventual electronic communication with Mr M as proposed by the mother.
In relation to the father’s time, the Independent Children’s Lawyer sought that the father’s current Saturday time arrangement be extended to six–hours but otherwise remain unchanged from the Final Orders. Similarly, the Independent Children’s Lawyer sought that the father be restrained from physically approaching or observing the mother.
Lastly, it was sought that the appointment of the Independent Children’s Lawyer be extended for 12–months, and that the parties pay the costs of the Independent Children’s Lawyer’s appearance for the trial.

ISSUES

The issues that remain to be determined by the Court are as follows:
Whether the Final Orders should be altered to remove the restraint on the mother from bringing the children into contact with Mr M;
If so, what spend time arrangements are appropriate as between Mr M and the children;
Whether the Final Orders should be altered to extend the time that the children spend with the father;
If so, whether such time should remain under professional supervision; and
Whether the father ought to pay one half of the costs of the Independent Children’s Lawyer’s attendance at the present trial.
The promotion of the safety of the children is the clear underlying issue in this case.

THE LAW

The applicable law is found in Part VII of the Act. The Family Law Amendment Act 2024 (Cth) (“the Amendment Act”) came into effect on 6 May 2024 and made substantive amendments to the previous Act which apply to these proceedings. In determining parenting matters under Part VII of the Act the Court must still regard the best interests of the child as the paramount consideration: s 60CA.
The objects and principles of Part VII are set out at s 60B:
60B Objects of Part

The objects of this Part are:
(a) to ensure that the best interests of children are met, including by ensuring their safety; and

(b) to give effect to the Convention on the Rights of the Child done at New York on 20 November 1989.

In regard to parental responsibility, the presumption of equal shared parental responsibility as created by the previous s 61DA of the Act has been abolished. Instead, s 61D(3) of the Act provides the Court with the ability to deal with the allocation of responsibility for making decisions in relation to children as follows:
61D Parenting orders and parental responsibility

...
(3) A parenting order that deals with the allocation of responsibility for making decisions about major long-term issues in relation to the child (see subsection 64B(3)) may provide for joint or sole decision-making in relation to all or specified major long-term issues.
Section 61DAA of the Act provides the Court with an understanding of what joint decision-making about major long-term issues entails:
61DAA Effect of parenting order that provides for joint decision-making about major long-term issues
(1) If a parenting order provides for joint decision-making by persons in relation to all or specified major long-term issues in relation to a child, then, except to the extent the order otherwise specifies, the order is taken to require each of the persons:
(a) to consult each other person in relation to each such decision; and

(b) to make a genuine effort to come to a joint decision.
(2) To avoid doubt, this section does not require any other person to establish, before acting on a decision about the child communicated by one of those persons, that the decision has been made jointly.
Subject to any order to the contrary, s 61DAB clarifies that if a child is spending time with a person under a parenting order, the order is not taken to require that person to consult with a person who has parental responsibility about decisions that are made in relation to the child that are not major long-term issues.
Because s 60CA and s 60B refer to the best interests of the child, the Court must consider s 60CC which specifies how the Court determines what is in a child’s best interests:
60CC How a court determines what is in a child’s best interests

Determining child's best interests
(1) Subject to subsection (4), in determining what is in the child’s best interests, the court must:
(a) consider the matters set out in subsection (2); and
(b) if the child is an Aboriginal or Torres Strait Islander child—also consider the matters set out in subsection (3).
General considerations
(2) For the purposes of paragraph (1)(a), the court must consider the following matters:
(a) what arrangements would promote the safety (including safety from being subjected to, or exposed to, family violence, abuse, neglect, or other harm) of:
(i) the child; and
(ii) each person who has care of the child (whether or not a person has parental responsibility for the child);
(b) any views expressed by the child;

(c) the developmental, psychological, emotional and cultural needs of the child;

(d) the capacity of each person who has or is proposed to have parental responsibility for the child to provide for the child’s developmental, psychological, emotional and cultural needs;

(e) the benefit to the child of being able to have a relationship with the child’s parents, and other people who are significant to the child, where it is safe to do so;

(f) anything else that is relevant to the particular circumstances of the child.

(2A) In considering the matters set out in paragraph (2)(a), the court must include consideration of:
(a) any history of family violence, abuse or neglect involving the child or a person caring for the child (whether or not the person had parental responsibility for the child); and

(b) any family violence order that applies or has applied to the child or a member of the child’s family.

(Emphasis in original)
The new legislative focus is on promoting the safety of children and carers, whereas the former provisions focused on protection from harm. The legislature must have intended there to be a distinction between the former provisions, which focused on protection from harm, and the current provisions which focus on the promotion of safety, otherwise the amendments would lack utility. As accepted by Parker J in Beumers & Stoica [2025] FedCFamC1F 695 at [44], following an earlier decision of this Court in Melounis & Melounis (No 4) [2024] FedCFamC1F 778, “the statutory requirement to promote safety involves advancing a positive outcome, which would necessarily include protection from harm but would not be limited to this”. The amendments require a more nuanced, proactive and future-focused approach to considering the safety of children and those who care for them. This will be guided by historical risk assessment.
As stated by Campton J in Barnabas & Phineus [2025] FedCFamC1A 215 at [17]:
The construction of Pt VII of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) (“the Act”) after the 2024 amendments reinforces the consideration of the safety of children or another person as a focused starting point in those considerations to be weighed in determining a parenting regime that best promotes the best interests of the children (Pierce & Pierce (No 2) [2025] FedCFamC1A 162 at [31]).

Emphasis added
Acknowledging this, the Court cautions against lapsing into simplistic binaries such as contending that promoting safety is always different from protection from harm. The reality is much more nuanced. Every case is different. Perhaps in many cases it will make no difference which approach is adopted, but in other cases the nuanced, proactive and future-focused difference is very important to the child and carer.

Family violence

The definition of family violence is found in s 4AB of the Act, reproduced below:
4AB Definition of family violence etc.
(1) For the purposes of this Act, family violence means violent, threatening or other behaviour by a person that coerces or controls a member of the person’s family (the family member) or causes the family member to be fearful.

(2) Examples of behaviour that may constitute family violence include (but are not limited to):

(a) an assault; or

(b) a sexual assault or other sexually abusive behaviour; or

(c) stalking; or

(d) repeated derogatory taunts; or

(e) intentionally damaging or destroying property; or

(f) intentionally causing death or injury to an animal; or
(g) unreasonably denying the family member the financial autonomy that he or she would otherwise have had; or

(h) unreasonably withholding financial support needed to meet the reasonable living expenses of the family member, or his or her child, at a time when the family member is entirely or predominantly dependent on the person for financial support; or

(i) preventing the family member from making or keeping connections with his or her family, friends or culture; or

(j) unlawfully depriving the family member, or any member of the family member’s family, of his or her liberty.

(3) For the purposes of this Act, a child is exposed to family violence if the child sees or hears family violence or otherwise experiences the effects of family violence.

(4) Examples of situations that may constitute a child being exposed to family violence include (but are not limited to) the child:

(a) overhearing threats of death or personal injury by a member of the child’s family towards another member of the child’s family; or

(b) seeing or hearing an assault of a member of the child’s family by another member of the child’s family; or

(c) comforting or providing assistance to a member of the child’s family who has been assaulted by another member of the child’s family; or

(d) cleaning up a site after a member of the child’s family has intentionally damaged property of another member of the child’s family; or

(e) being present when police or ambulance officers attend an incident involving the assault of a member of the child’s family by another member of the child’s family.

The section is both remedial and protective and as such should not be read down by artificial limitations (Project Blue Sky Inc v Australian Broadcasting Authority (1998) 194 CLR 355).
Disputed allegations of family violence must be subjected to the same forensic rigour as any other contested factual issue (Edinger & Duy [2023] FedCFamC1A 194; (2023) 68 Fam LR 55). The party alleging the fact bears the burden of proving it (Wallaby Grip Ltd v QBE Insurance (Australia) Ltd (2010) 240 CLR 444 at [36]) and the standard of the burden is the balance of probabilities (s 140 of the Evidence Act 1995 (Cth) (“Evidence Act”)). The same point was recently made by the Full Court, though perhaps in less robust terms (Leventis & Leventis [2024] FedCFamC1A 141; (2024) FLC 94-204 at [13]–[19]).

Unacceptable risk

The High Court of Australia (“The High Court”) in M v M (1988) 166 CLR 69 (“M v M”), at [76] said:
...the ultimate and paramount issue to be decided in proceedings for custody of, or access to, a child is whether the making of the order sought is in the interest of the welfare of the child. The fact that the proceedings involve an allegation that the child has been sexually abuse by the parent whom seeks custody or access does not alter the paramount and ultimate issue which the court has to determine, though the court’s findings on the disputed allegation of sexual abuse will naturally have an important, perhaps a decisive impact, on the resolution of that issue.
Further, the High Court states at [77] – [78]:
No doubt there will be some cases in which the court is able to come to a positive finding that the allegation is well founded. In all but the most extraordinary cases, that finding will have a decisive impact on the order to be made respecting custody and access. There will be cases also in which the court has no hesitation in rejecting the allegation as groundless. Again, in the nature of things there will be very many cases, such as the present case, in which the court cannot confidently make a finding that sexual abuse has taken place. And there are strong practical family reasons why the court should refrain from making a positive finding that sexual abuse has actually taken place unless it is impelled by the particular circumstances of the case to do so.

In resolving the wider issue the court must determine whether on the evidence there is a risk of sexual abuse occurring if custody or access be granted and assess the magnitude of that risk. After all, in deciding what is in the best interests of a child, the Family Court is frequently called upon to assess and evaluate the likelihood or possibility of events or occurrences which, if they come about, will have a detrimental impact on the child's welfare. The existence and magnitude of the risk of sexual abuse, as with other risks of harm to the welfare of a child, is a fundamental matter to be taken into account in deciding issues of custody and access. In access cases, the magnitude of the risk may be less if the order in contemplation is supervised access. Even in such a case, however, there may be a risk of disturbance to a child who is compulsorily brought into contact with a parent who has sexually abused her or whom the child believes to have sexually abused her. But that is not the issue in this case.

Efforts to define with greater precision the magnitude of the risk which will justify a court in denying a parent access to a child have resulted in a variety of formulations. The degree of risk has been described as a "risk of serious harm" (A. v. A.), "an element of risk" or "an appreciable risk" (Marriage of M.), "a real possibility" (B. v. B. (Access)), a "real risk" (Leveque v. Leveque), and an "unacceptable risk": In re G. (A minor). This imposing array indicates that the courts are striving for a greater degree of definition than the subject is capable of yielding. In devising these tests the courts have endeavoured, in their efforts to protect the child's paramount interests, to achieve a balance between the risk of detriment to the child from sexual abuse and the possibility of benefit to the child from parental access. To achieve a proper balance, the test is best expressed by saying that a court will not grant custody or access to a parent if that custody or access would expose the child to an unacceptable risk of sexual abuse.
In Isles & Nelissen [2022] FedCFamC1A 97; (2022) FLC 94-092 (Isles & Nelissen), the Full Court of this Court reconsidered the issue of unacceptable risk. In the course of their reasons, their Honours observed that unacceptable risk is not to be established on the balance of probabilities.
Simply put, the Court is required to make factual findings about past relevant disputed events underpinning the allegations, to the requisite standard of proof and then on the basis of those findings and other relevant considerations, predict the level of future risk to a child. In carrying out the risk assessment, the Court assesses both the prediction of future harm and the severity of the impact, if such harm were to eventuate. The possibility of future harm and severity of harm may be determined as unacceptable.
Their Honours at [50], agreed with and adopted the commentary of Austin J in Fitzwater v Fitzwater [2019] FamCAFC 251; (2019) 60 Fam LR 212 (“Fitzwater”). At [132]–[142] of Fitzwater, his Honour observed:
Having eschewed the need for any positive factual finding that the father had sexually abused the eldest child, the mother’s case was instead that the evidence was still sufficiently persuasive to establish the risk of sexual abuse posed by the father to the children, and furthermore, that the magnitude of the risk was unacceptably high, such that it could only be attenuated by the imposition of the children’s permanent supervision when spending time with the father. The trial judge properly understood that to be the nature of the mother’s case, as is evident from the reasons for judgment (at [1], [31], [60], [497], [501]). Consequently, the mother and the trial judge were entirely focussed on what the evidence implied was the risk of harm to the children in the future; not what probably did or did not happen in the past.
In civil proceedings, s 140(1) of the Evidence Act provides the “case of a party” must be found proven if the court is satisfied of its proof on the balance of probabilities. For that purpose, the “case of a party” is defined (in the Dictionary to the Evidence Act) to mean “the facts in issue in respect of which the party bears the legal burden of proof”. The substantive law determines where the legal burden of proof falls in respect of facts in issue.
It must be borne in mind that proceedings in respect of children under Part VII of the Act, while civil in nature, are not disputes inter partes in the ordinary sense of that expression because the court is not enforcing a parental right to custody or access (M v M at 76; ZP v PS [1994] HCA 29; (1994) 181 CLR 639 at 647). The paramount consideration in Part VII proceedings is the child’s best interests (ss 60CA, 65AA of the Act). Unlike in other forms of civil litigation, no party bears an onus of proving the factual elements of a common law, equitable, or statutory cause of action to justify an entitlement to remedy. Rather, each party adduces evidence and propounds a suite of orders which he or she contends meets the child’s best interests, which gives the proceedings a different character (CDJ v VAJ at 188-189). The resultant orders represent the court’s discretionary judgment about how the child’s interests will be best served and, due to the sheer breadth of the discretion, two judges may, with complete integrity and upon the same material, come to differing conclusions (CDJ v VAJ at 231).
The conclusion reached by a court in Part VII proceedings, as reflected in the decrees it makes, is still premised upon proof of relevant facts and circumstances by evidence, but the law draws a distinction between proof of historical facts and the prediction of future possibilities. In determining what did or did not happen in the past, a court decides on the balance of probabilities, but not when hypothesising about future possibilities (Malec v J.C. Hutton Pty Ltd [1990] HCA 20; (1990) 169 CLR 638 (“Malec”)).
In Malec, Brennan and Dawson JJ said (at 639-640):
...facts of that [historical] kind are ascertained for the purposes of civil litigation on the balance of probabilities...the ascertainment of [future] earning capacity involves an evaluation of possibilities, not establishing a fact as a matter of history...the court must form an estimate of the likelihood that the possibility will occur...

...

...To make a finding on the balance of probabilities as though the prospect were something that had occurred in the past was to misconceive the process of evaluation...
and Deane, Gaudron and McHugh JJ said (at 643):
...The future may be predicted and the hypothetical may be conjectured... Where proof is necessarily unattainable, it would be unfair to treat as certain a prediction which has a 51 per cent probability of occurring, but to ignore altogether a prediction which has a 49 per cent probability of occurring...
The High Court was there referring to the prediction of a plaintiff’s income earning capacity in the context of quantifying personal injury damages, but the principle has been applied just as aptly to predictions about the risk of harm to children in this jurisdiction (see Oswald & Karrington (2016) FLC 93-726 at [60]; Bant & Clayton [2015] FamCAFC 222; (2015) 53 Fam LR 621 at [99], [107], [171], [172]). Such application of principle is consistent with M v M.
The assessment of risk is a predictive exercise and while it is, naturally enough, liable to be influenced by factual findings about past events, the contemplation of risk entails the foresight of possible harm. It is an oddity to expect that the mere possibility of future harm can or should be proven as a probability, as has been implied before (Potter and Potter [2007] FamCA 350; (2007) FLC 93-326 at [110], [129]). Risks of harm must be heeded even if they are improbable eventualities.
Speaking of the risk of some future occurrence is just another way of expressing the chance of it happening. The concept of chance lies along a continuum, encompassing all outcomes which lie in the range between highly probable and remotely possible, assuming the polar extremes of certainty are ignored. In the current context, the higher the chance of the children’s sexual abuse, the greater the risk of their physical or psychological harm. At some point on the continuum the risk of such harm becomes so potent it cannot be tolerated: it is unacceptable.
It cannot be correct that the unacceptable risk of a child’s sufferance of harm through future sexual abuse can only ever be established if it is proven as a fact, on the balance of probabilities, that the child (or another) has already been sexually abused in the past. Depending upon the strength of the evidence placed before the court, the possibility of past sexual abuse may of itself be sufficient to establish the chance of future sexual abuse. That has long been accepted as true (Nikolakis & Nikolakis [2010] FamCAFC 52 at [41], [44], [49]-[53], [96]; Partington & Cade (No.2) [2009] FamCAFC 230; (2009) FLC 93-422 at [56]- [61]; Johnson and Page at [68], [71], [76], [77]).
Indeed, that was exactly the factual scenario in M v M. There, the trial judge concluded it was possible the father had sexually abused the subject child, but could not make a positive finding it had occurred on the balance of probabilities, and therefore discharged the “access” order (as it was then described) to eliminate the future risk of the child being sexually abused by the father. The father’s appeal was dismissed because the possibility of the child’s past sexual abuse was sufficient, on the evidence adduced in that case, to establish the unacceptably high risk of the child’s future sexual abuse.
As was recognised by Hale LJ (as her Ladyship then was) in Re C and B (Children) (Care Order: Future Harm) [2001] 1 F.L.R 611 at [28], in child-related proceedings, a comparatively small risk of really serious harm can justify action, while even the virtual certainty of slight harm might not. It could hardly be otherwise, because no prudent adult would willingly expose a child to the risk of sexual abuse when there is an unacceptably high chance of its occurrence, even though the chance is not proven by the evidence to be probable. Requiring the proof of any possible future child abuse as a probability would pervert the law as settled by the High Court in M v M and Malec.
In Eastley & Eastley [2022] FedCFamC1A 101; (2022) FLC 94-094 (“Eastley”), the Full Court observed that a trial judge must consider the cumulative effect of the whole of the evidence to assess the level of risk posed to a child, rather than dealing with individual events in isolation at [33]:
The first point to be made is that the law did not require the primary judge to assess the potency of the risk of harm posed to the children by reference to the evidence concerning individual events in isolation from the remainder of the evidence. On the contrary, the primary judge was required to assess the level of risk posed to the children on the whole of the evidence, since the strength of the evidence lies in its cumulative effect, much like how the strength of rope derives from the combination of its individually weaker strands (Transport Industries Insurance Co Ltd v Longmuir [1997] 1 VR 125 at 127–130 and 141; Savage v Lunn [1998] NSWCA 203; J.D Heydon, Cross on Evidence (LexisNexis Australia, 13th edition, 2021) at [1110] and [9040]).
This is a case where there are serious allegations of grooming. The meaning of this was considered by Austin J in Ridley & Ridley (No. 2) [2020] FamCA 549 at [59].
Before discussing the relevant evidence, it is instructive to explain the meaning of “grooming”, by quoting the Family Consultant:
209. The act of sexual assault commonly involves an element of ‘grooming’. Grooming is a tactic which involves a subtle, escalating process of establishing trust with an intended child victim and then seeking to desensitise that child to sexual conduct. The violation of boundaries is an important element in the grooming process and may include acts such as showering or dressing with a child. Offenders often desensitise a child to touch by beginning with non-sexual touching such as tickling or stroking, before progressing to sexual touching.

210. Grooming can involve behaviours that may not seem to be inappropriate. This enables offenders to groom others, and the environment, as other adults will commonly defend their behaviour as ‘innocent’ acts. Grooming the environment can often involve offenders referring to past/current or intended victims as “liars”, or unstable, problematic children, to discredit any disclosures and generate an environment of disbelief around the child. Grooming creates opportunities for offenders to progress to acts of abuse.

THE EXPERT EVIDENCE

The expert evidence in this case is the Single Joint Expert Report; Exhibit ICL6. The Single Joint Expert Report was based on interviews with the parents, the maternal grandmother, and the children that occurred on 26 July 2024. The parents participated in follow up interviews on 5 August 2024 and Mr M was interviewed on 7 August 2024.
All references within this section should be construed as referring to the Single Joint Expert Report unless explicitly stated otherwise. The Court notes both that, the Single Joint Expert did not have the benefit of the parent’s trial material at the time of drafting the Single Joint Expert Report and, of course, did not have the benefit the Court enjoys of access to the totality of the evidence at the hearing, including the cross-examination of both the parents and Mr M.
Before considering the Single Joint Expert Report it is noted that at the time of his interview, the father did not have a definitive proposal for the Single Joint Expert to consider. He simply noted that he wanted “what is best for the children” and that he would be “happy to take them full time it’s deemed by the Judge, ICL, that the children are at risk and feel the children will be safe with me on a fulltime basis” (paragraph 33).

The mother’s capacity to identify risk

The mother deposed that the messages between Mr M and the young person were “terrible, disgusting, inappropriate” and that she understands how they appear to be grooming (paragraph 49). Knowledge of these messages first became apparent “a year and a bit” into the relationship between the mother and Mr M. The Court understands this to be sometime within the first quarter of 2020. The mother alleges that the father had knowledge of the text messages in September 2020 and suggests that he deliberately waited to bring an application until 22 December 2022 to “ruin Christmas” (paragraph 50). Of course, no finding can be made on this point having regard to the evidence before the Court.
The mother and Mr M have attended a Child Grooming and Sexual Abuse course held by PP Organisation and the NSW Police Force in early 2024 (“Child Grooming and Sexual Abuse course”) (Exhibit ICL7) and have attended upon Dr CC for assessment on 4 October 2023. Further, individually, the mother has attended upon Ms EE in compliance with the Final Orders.
The Single Joint Expert acknowledged that the mother was the victim of coercive control by the father during the relationship (paragraph 207). That was a finding previously made by this Court. The mother’s relationship with Mr M was described by the Single Joint Expert as “rapid” having only separated from the father six–months prior to her engagement with Mr M. This called into question the mother’s “attachment style, and the potential for her psychological and safety needs to be met by older men” (paragraph 209).
Throughout the Single Joint Expert Report interviews, the mother maintained that Mr M was “innocent of the allegation of sexually grooming a minor”. The Court notes that such maintenance of Mr M’s innocence occurs after this Court made findings about those messages, conceded to be sent by Mr M. The mother clearly knew about such findings. This Court has never found that Mr M physically assaulted the young person. The finding was that, whether intended or not, Mr M’s messages to the young person, when read objectively, had characteristics consistent with sexually grooming a minor. In these circumstances the mother maintaining that Mr M is “innocent of the allegation” (emphasis added) demonstrates a persistent denial of the findings of this Court. Indeed, it may even demonstrate that the “blinkers” never truly came off the mother’s eyes. In seeming alignment with this view, the Single Joint Expert opined that “this may reflect confidence in her understanding of [Mr M], bias in believing his innocence, and/or an impaired incapacity to identify problematic relational dynamics” (paragraph 211). The mother is determined to “prove to the Court that she has matured from her former relationship” (paragraph 212).
The Single Joint Expert noted that it is important for the mother to remain engaged in psychological counselling to continue her self-understanding and to continue to protect the children from potential harm (paragraph 214). The recommendations of the Single Joint Expert advocate for Mr M to spend progressive time with the children over a period of 12–months, firstly by method of professionally supervised contact, then contact supervised by the mother (paragraph 259). This recommendation places the mother in the position to report any risk identified.
During cross-examination the Single Joint Expert confirmed that she did “hold some reservations about whether...[the mother] would be able to step up and protect the children should there be risk” (Transcript 3 September 2025, p.12 line 35). It was put to the Single Joint Expert that she would be concerned about the mother’s ability to report risk given that the level of financial support Mr M provides the mother, and she agreed that she “can see how that conclusion can be drawn, yes” (Transcript 3 September 2025, p.13 line 40). Additionally, the Single Joint Expert agreed that it is possible the mother may have reservations in relation to reporting any risk of Mr M due to the loss of emotional support (Transcript 3 September 2025, p.14 line 5).
During the final hearing the Single Joint Expert was taken through the mother’s proposal in relation to the potential reintegration of Mr M into the children’s life, and she confirmed that her recommendations are contingent upon the Court determining Mr M is no longer a risk (Transcript 3 September 2025, p.15 line 30).
As will be seen below, based on the totality of the evidence, the Court finds both that Mr M remains an unacceptable risk to the safety of the children, and that the mother remains oblivious to this.

Any potential risk posed by Mr M

The Single Joint Expert agrees that Mr M is “limited in insight and self-reflective capacity and with unsophisticated reasoning. This may explain his superficial explanation about his inappropriate communication” (paragraph 240). In relation to the assessment of sexual risk of harm Mr M poses to the children, at paragraph 244 the Single Joint Expert said:
I am reserved about the assessment of sexual abuse risk. As identified accurately by [Dr CC], the assessment of risk of sexual abuse was erroneously coded by the former two forensic experts who utilised the STATIC-99R. My concern is the characterisation of the communication with the child as a “mistake” and the suggestion that this communication was in the service of the child’s father who was seeking more information. The lack of accountability for the communication is concerning as there is no real understanding of why the inappropriate conversation occurred, and what may have followed had the minor decided to meet with [Mr M].

(Emphasis in original).
During cross-examination the Single Joint Expert confirmed that the use of the STATIC–99R test was incorrect in the circumstances of this case because Mr M was a non-contact and non-charged offender (Transcript 3 September 2025, p.11 lines 10–20). Because of this, she confirmed that the risk of recidivism of Mr M remains unknown due to the absence of a proper assessment tool being implemented (Transcript 3 September 2025, p.11 lines 25-30).
Despite the possible sexual risk Mr M may pose, the Single Joint Expert noted that his behaviours “evidenced parenting responsibility and capacity to place the children’s needs as priority” (paragraph 245). Ultimately, the Single Joint Expert recommended a progressive time regime for Mr M to be reintroduced into the children’s lives (paragraphs 259–261). However, a significant limitation of the Single Joint Expert Report is that the recommendations provided are on an interim basis (Transcript 3 September 2025, p.11 lines 35–40).
For reasons that will become apparent below, the Court does not accept the recommendation of the Single Joint Expert about the children spending time with Mr M, and finds, based on the totality of the evidence before it, that Mr M is an unacceptable risk to the safety of the children.

The parenting capacity of the father

The Single Joint Expert Report writer noted that the father was “vague and evaded crucial points” (paragraph 217). It was noted that the father “forgave everybody in the system” (paragraph 217). The Single Joint Expert commented that the father “did not acknowledge his contribution to the family violence or take responsibility for the hurt he caused” (paragraph 218). The father was unable to acknowledge that his continued behaviour can still cause fear for the mother, and that this demonstrated inability to acknowledge is reflective of his “low levels of understanding and insight about his contributions to the requirement for supervised time with his children” (paragraph 228). During cross-examination, the Single Joint Expert confirmed that she remains concerned about the father’s level of insight (Transcript 3 September 2025, p.19 lines 40–45) and agreed that the father’s proposal to spend immediate unsupervised time with the children is premature (Transcript 3 September 2025, p.24 line 15).
Despite having ample opportunity to engage in post-separation parenting interventions, the father only submitted applications for the same one month prior to the Single Joint Expert Report interviews. Similarly, despite having authority to directly liaise with the children’s treaters and school since 31 March 2022, the father remains “unclear about the children’s developmental conditions, and intervention for these conditions” (paragraph 220). The Court agrees that there has been a consistent low level of engagement on the part of the father to “follow and support the children’s developmental progress outside of the supervised visits” (paragraph 221). In effect, the father has “chosen to abdicate his role as parenting by leaving [the mother] to solely manage the children’s special needs, education and extra-curricular activities” and he justifies his limited engagement because the mother has the primary care of the children (paragraph 230).
The Single Joint Expert opined that the father’s way of thinking is “transactional and not child-focused” (paragraph 230). The Court, again, agrees. The Single Joint Expert confirmed during cross-examination that significant concern remains in relation to the father’s lack of engagement with the children’s treaters and health professionals (Transcript 3 September 2025, p.23 line 5).
Similarly, the father’s financial contributions to the children have also been limited. At paragraph 231 the Single Joint Expert noted:
Parental responsibility of children is experienced by children when they are aware of a parent’s presence through emotional connection and understanding of their interests, engagement with their medical and neurodevelopmental conditions, and through an overall sense that the parent is interested and motivated to contribute to their well-being. A financial commitment to the well-being of [X] and [Y] can be through their engagement in extra-curricular activities, financial responsibility of their treatments. A stable and long-term demonstration of his capacity to take on his parental role and responsibilities can deepen [the father’s] connection with his children and potentially lead to an increased presence in their lives.
The Single Joint Expert confirmed that the father views the financial settlement between the parents as his “financial contribution to the children” (Transcript 3 September 2025, p. 21 lines 35–40).
During observations undertaken by the Single Joint Expert during the reporting process, the interactions between the father and children were noted to be “warm, playful and mutually enjoyed”. The Single Joint Expert opined that the father can “meaningfully engage with the children for short periods of time” (paragraph 230). It was confirmed that the children’s experience with the father may be enhanced by allowing their time together to occur in a public place “like a cinema or even a playground or a park” but reiterated that it ought to remain supervised (Transcript 3 September 2025, p. 29 lines 5–10). Ultimately, the Single Joint Expert opined that the father “has not demonstrated increased engagement in the children’s care beyond the three hour supervised visits” (paragraph 232). Based on the totality of the evidence before the Court, the Court agrees.
The Single Joint Expert recommended that the father engage in professional therapeutic support for at least ten–months to increase his understanding of his contributions to family violence, and to further to increase his engagement with the parental responsibilities associated with the children’s academic and health functioning. Despite having ample time to implement the recommendations of the Single Joint Expert, the father has not done so.

REPORTS OF DR CC

The mother has requested on four separate occasions that Dr CC, a clinical and forensic psychologist, provide a report for these proceedings in relation to herself and Mr M’s relationship in the context of the grooming allegations. All reports provided were annexed to Dr CC’s affidavit and formed Exhibit C1.
A brief summary of each report will be provided below and all references within this section should be construed as referring to the report identified in each sub-heading unless explicitly stated otherwise. The Court notes both that Dr CC did not have the benefit of the mother’s and Mr M’s trial material at the time of the report and, of course, did not have the benefit the Court enjoys of access to the totality of the evidence at the hearing, including the cross-examination of both the mother and Mr M.

Report dated 4 October 2023

Dr CC noted that Mr M “impressed as a rather unsophisticated individual intellectually and much more so than [the mother] and this was evident in his capacity for insight and reflection” (paragraph 15). In relation to any discussion pertaining to the children, the discussion was led by the mother, and she did not seek any approval or guidance from Mr M prior to answering (paragraph 20). The mother spoke dotingly of Mr M and said that he makes her feel “happy from the inside out”, that he is the “perfect person”, and a “great role model” for her children (paragraph 31). The mother was not able to identify anything negative about Mr M (paragraph 34). The Court, again, questions how the mother came to the conclusion that Mr M was a “perfect person” knowing that this Court has found that he sexually groomed the young person. Indeed, a strong inference of the mother’s response is that she, consciously, or subconsciously, denies the veracity of the past findings of this Court.
Specifically in relation to the grooming incident, the mother suggested that Mr M did not know “what he was getting himself into” and conceded that the communication would “appear to be grooming ‘without the backstory’, as the text messages ‘aren’t appropriate’” (paragraph 60). The Court notes that the mother’s narrative to Dr CC accords with that later expressed to the Single Joint Expert, although perhaps in less definitive terms. The mother appears to fail to recognise that the messages sent by Mr M more than merely appear to be grooming, but have been found to be grooming. To suggest anything otherwise would be to challenge a finding of this Court not subject to appeal.
The justification provided by Mr M was that the young person’s father held a concern that his child was “transitioning” gender, “engaged in sex work” and that the content of his communication with the young person was intentionally designed to “see how far (he) could’ve pushed” (paragraph 61-62). Mr M advised Dr CC that although he was never charged with any criminal offence in relation to the grooming incident, he was investigated by the authorities over a period of six-weeks, and no crime was identified (paragraph 63). The Court notes that such investigation is relevant, but not dispositive, and was considered previously by this Court.
The mother advised that she has not “seen any red flags” in relation to Mr M. When probed further by Dr CC about what some red flags may look like, the mother was able to identify some accurate examples. The mother told Dr CC that she believes the Court has considered her to be “dumb and blind sided” due to her relationship with the father and that she has “a pattern” where she gets involved with older men where she is vulnerable (paragraph 66).
Prior to the Final Orders, Mr M had an active involvement with the children and resembled a “father figure” for the children (paragraph 44). The mother suggested that X holds Mr M up “on a pedestal” (paragraph 49).
Mr M advised Dr CC that he has attended upon Mr JJ and Mr GG, who have assessed his risk of sexual offending. During this process, Mr M reported that he learned “that he “fucked up” and should not “get involved”, that he was “vulnerable” and that he has “learned (his) mistakes” (paragraphs 55–56).
The mother advised that Y’s expressive and receptive language skills have improved considerably with time and professional intervention, such that she does not consider him to be as vulnerable anymore (paragraph 73). For reasons that will be canvased below, the Court questions this contention.
Both the mother and Mr M completed the Paulhus Deception Scale (PDS), which is “a self-report measure that is designed to explore a person’s tendency to distort test profiles through socially desirable responding” (paragraph 79). The mother’s PDS scores indicate “she is likely to lack insight into problem areas... [the mother] likely engaged in a degree of positive impression management that may raise doubt about her approach to the assessment and the veracity of her account at interview” (paragraph 80). The scores recorded for Mr M accorded with that of the mother (paragraph 81).
Further, both the mother and Mr M completed the Personality Assessment Inventory (PAI) which is a “a self-administered and objective measure of both personality and psychopathological variables” (paragraph 82). Both scored exceptionally high for impression management – such that it rendered the test profile invalid. This suggests “considerable defensiveness in responding” (paragraph 83). Of note, Mr GG also administered the PAI and commented that the results indicated that “there is slight elevation on measures of persecution, as Mr M feels he has been treated inequitably in light of Family Court rulings” (paragraph 100). Lastly, Mr M’s cognitive functioning was assessed using the Ravens Standard Progressive Matrice. Mr M’s cognitive capacity “is below 91% of his aged peers” which suggests that his insight and self-reflective capacity is limited in addition to his reasoning being unsophisticated (paragraph 87).
Dr CC opined that the explanation Mr M gave was:
Somewhat difficult to accept but is not implausible and it is consistent with the text message exchange between [Mr M] and the child’s father. I am also of the view that [Mr M]’s reasoning in relation to his conduct is superficial and clearly inappropriate but commensurate with his intellectual capacity.

(paragraph 110).
Additionally, in relation to the outcome of psychological intervention Dr CC opined:
[Mr M] reported gaining from psychological intervention, although these gains are seemingly superficial and limited. I understand this as a function of both the lack of required treatment agenda, in addition to [Mr M]’s limited cognitive and reflective capacity.

(paragraph 113).
The Court places no weight on Dr CC’s opinion of plausibility of Mr M’s explanation of the grooming incident or whether he has benefitted from the psychological intervention. Ultimately, these are findings for the Court to make, not an Expert. As will become apparent, the Court will find that his explanation of the grooming incident is implausible, and that the psychological intervention he has received has not benefited him or minimised the risk he presents to the children’s safety.

Report dated 10 October 2023

Dr CC clarified for the parties what Mr M’s score on the Ravens Standard Progressive Matrice indicates:
[A] score below the 10th percentile would certainly be reflective of someone who was low functioning intellectually and this is commensurate with [Mr M]’s presentation at interview. Someone who functions at this level would likely find it challenging to negotiate complex or abstract tasks or concepts, in addition to which their reasoning skills are likely to be superficial and fail to take into account moral or ethical issues. Further to this, someone who functions at this level would likely find it difficult to take the perspective of others and their decision making is more likely to be impulsive and poorly reasoned. They may also struggle to think consequentially at other than a basic level.

(page 4).

Report dated 25 July 2024

Dr CC confirmed at the time of writing her report dated 4 October 2023 she had not been provided with a copy of the text message exchange between Mr M and the young person. However, after reading the text message exchange, Dr CC confirmed that her opinion in relation the mother’s and Mr M’s relationship had not changed (paragraph 7).
In relation to the explanation provided Mr M for the grooming incident, Dr CC opined:
... whilst this account is difficult to accept and quite unpalatable, it is not impossible or necessarily implausible. I do believe that someone of [Mr M]’s social skill and intellectual capacity may have engaged in such communication with the understanding that he was responding to a request from the child's father to find out information relevant to concerns he had for his son. However, equally, it may be that [Mr M]'s account to this effect is fabricated and that he was sexualising the relationship with the child and seeking to groom him for sexual purposes. Even if this is the case however, it would be inappropriate to assume that because [Mr M] groomed a 13 year old boy via text messages, that he would pose a risk of sexual abuse to [the] children.

(paragraph 7).
The Court respectfully notes the professional opinion of Dr CC but disagrees. Based on the totality of the evidence, the Court finds that the account of Mr M is implausible and that he was, in fact, grooming the young person. The Court is aware of, and has considered, the assessments of the intellectual capacity of Mr M, indeed of the mother as well. The hypothesis that this factor explains the deficiencies in their evidence has been considered but is rejected. As will be seen, there is an element of calculation in the acts and omissions of both Mr M and the mother which are, the Court finds, not explained by low intellectual capacity. In any event, the focus of the Court is on the promotion of the safety of children in all circumstances, whether or not a parental significant person in their lives has a low intellectual capacity.

Report dated 28 July 2025

Once again, Dr CC presented an opinion on the motivation of Mr M for engaging in the grooming incident:
I have indicated previously that this account seems unlikely and unpalatable but it is not impossible that this was, in fact, the case for [Mr M], especially when one considers his unsophisticated intellectual capacity, leading to poor reasoning and difficulties appreciating complex and abstract concepts.

(paragraph 8(b)).
The Court observes that Dr CC’s assessment of Mr M’s explanation evolved from “somewhat difficult to accept but is not implausible and it is consistent” and “is difficult to accept and quite unpalatable, it is not impossible or necessarily implausible” to “seems unlikely and unpalatable but it is not impossible”. Seemingly, after being confronted by the text message exchange between Mr M and the young person, Dr CC’s opinion weakens in terms of her acceptance of Mr M’s explanation. In any event, as expressed above, the Court places no weight on Dr CC’s opinion of plausibility of Mr M’s explanation of the grooming incident.

THE MOTHER’S EVIDENCE

Given the long litigation history of this case, and mother being on (either actual or constructive) notice of the Court’s concerns about whether or not she retained a “blinkered” view about the risks posed by Mr M to the children, one would have expected the mother to clearly and unhesitatingly demonstrate through, not only the content of her evidence, but how that evidence was given, that the “blinkers” were indeed off. Unfortunately, she failed to do so. Her oral evidence was characterised by repeatedly failing to make logical, sensible, and harmless concessions which, regrettably, created an overwhelming impression of defensiveness which eroded the Court’s confidence in her evidence.
The evidence provided by the mother at times also appeared evasive and inconsistent. The Court recognises that this is consistent with her PAI and Paulhus scores as noted by Dr CC in her Report dated 4 October 2023. The effect of this being such that the mother impression manages in a manner which may result in “considerable defensiveness in responding” (Report of Dr CC dated 4 October 2023, paragraph 83).

Y’s vulnerability

Y’s capacity to communicate any behaviour of concern from Mr M to the mother, and the mother’s insight about Y’s deficits, are important issues for the Court. The mother’s evidence on this point causes the Court further concern.
Y’s diagnosis of Autism Spectrum Disorder (“ASD”) was not disputed. During cross-examination the mother confirmed that Y’s ASD diagnosis had been reduced to level 2, and he is now verbal. When it was put to the mother that Y’s speech is “a lower level of dialogue” and that he might not “speak in completely full sentences”, the mother was only able to agree “to a degree” (Transcript 1 September 2025, p.5 lines 25-30).
Similarly, despite the mother agreeing that on occasion X communicates on Y’s behalf, the mother was only able to accept “to a degree” that what X may interpret and reiterate may not actually be what Y was expressing (Transcript 1 September 2025, p.6 lines 5-30). The mother was not able to fully accept what was a plausible proposition that, plainly put, an eleven-year-old may not be able to interpret with accuracy an eight-year-old-child’s incomplete sentence. It is highly likely, the Court finds, that Y’s capacity to report any harm he experiences or is exposed to, is compromised by his ASD.
Despite having Ms KK, Y’s Speech Therapist, file an affidavit on 25 August 2025, the annexures to such affidavit were not tendered, and thus the Court is limited in terms of objective evidence to corroborate the mother’s evidence. The Single Joint Expert noted that during the assessment which occurred on 26 July 2024 “[Y] spoke in incomplete sentences and communicated his thoughts adequately” and also “used facial expressions and gestures to communicate” (Single Joint Expert Report, paragraph 20). The Court notes that what the Single Joint Expert meant by “adequately” in the present context is unclear. Additionally, the Single Joint Expert stated that Y was “easily distractable and was unable to directly respond to some questions posted to him” (Single Joint Expert Report, paragraph 159). In contrast, on 4 October 2023, some nine months earlier, the mother reported to Dr CC that “[Y]’s expressive and receptive language skills have improved considerably with time and professional intervention, such that she does not consider him to be as vulnerable anymore” (Report of Dr CC dated 4 October 2023, paragraph 73).
The Court finds that the mother’s impression of Y’s vulnerability concerning. All other material before the Court casts doubts on the mother’s confidence. In the Report of Dr CC dated 4 October 2023, Dr CC states at paragraph two that “I understand that [Y] has autism spectrum disorder (ASD), as a result of which his functioning is seriously impaired, in addition not which he is ostensibly non-verbal”. Further, in the Report of Dr CC dated 28 July 2025 at paragraph 8(f), regardless as to her belief of Mr M’s motivations, that “Notwithstanding, given his past behaviour, it would be prudent for Ms. Fontain to be considered in relation to her management of the children and their relationship with Mr. M, especially given her son's vulnerabilities”. To the extent that the information present before Dr CC allowed, as of three months prior to the final hearing before the Court, “vulnerabilities” were noted and caution was called for.
Whilst Y may have improved, and he may be less vulnerable, the mother’s resistance to acknowledging the true limits in his current level of verbal functioning is disconcerting to the Court. If her view about this is overly optimistic, she will likely be less cautious about whether Y does have capacity to self-protect via making disclosures of any inappropriate conduct by Mr M. Just as Y’s vulnerability is accentuated by deficits, so too is his mother’s lack of insight compromised by her inability or unwillingness to accept the safety concerns presented by Mr M. Moreover, the Court cannot rule out the alternative to the above – that the mother is simply prevaricating about the matters put to her as would align with her tendency to positively manage impression.
Curiously, X herself identified to the Single Joint Expert that Y is vulnerable and requires protection (Expert Report, paragraph 201). The Court agrees.

The children’s memories of Mr M

Another example of the mother refusing to accept a plausible proposition was when she disagreed that it is “unlikely that [Y] would have independent memories of what occurred at two [...] years old” in relation to Mr M (Transcript 1 September 2025, p.7 lines 30–40). Further, it was put to the mother that “any ‘memories’ that [Y] has [of Mr M], it’s only because [the mother] have told him about [Mr M]” and the mother disagreed with this proposition (Transcript 1 September 2025, p. 58 line 5). Despite the mother providing such evidence during her cross-examination, she advised the Single Joint Expert that “[Mr M] is kept alive in conversations” (Single Joint Expert Report, paragraph 66).

Other issues arising from the mother’s cross-examination

Examples from the mother’s cross-examination of failing to make logical, sensible and sometimes harmless concessions included the following:
The mother declining to accept that X adopts a motherly role in relation to Y, sometimes communicating on his behalf, and that this could be inappropriate from a parenting perspective (Transcript 1 September 2025, p. 4 line to p.5 line 20).
In relation to the Child Grooming and Sexual Abuse course that the mother attended in 2024, after accepting that she learnt about the “red flags” associated with grooming behaviour, she declined to unequivocally accept that those “red flags” were very similar to the messages that Mr M had sent to the young person (Transcript 1 September 2025, p. 21 line 10-15). Indeed, the Court finds that there was a striking similarity between the “red flags” which the mother purportedly learnt about and the communication between Mr M and the young person.
When cross-examined about her knowledge of these communications, which the mother knew or should have known was critical evidence in this case, she demonstrated an intriguing reluctance to accept certain facts. For example, the mother’s answers repeatedly infer that there were other messages not before the Court – which is a contention forming no part of her case (Transcript 1 September 2025, p.22 lines 31-42).
