For example, even in the evidence of Mr M, there is an acceptance that he initiated the communication with the young person, and not the reverse. It is almost as if the mother had an alternate theory about what occurred, which was unsupported by, indeed inconsistent with, the evidence.
The mother’s palpable (but inexplicable) defensiveness about the communications in question is evident (Transcript 1 September 2025, p. 26 lines 12–19). From the Court’s perspective, the mother’s reticence to quickly concede the matters put to her about the communication undermines its confidence about her own protective capacity in relation to Mr M.
Another example of this is found at Transcript 1 September 2025, p.27 lines 16–31, which states:
... And the response from [Mr M]is:

Are you a cheeky monkey?

?‑‑‑Yes.

Initially, just on a bare reading, ma’am, and being objective, you would agree that’s a concerning thing to respond to, saying – when somebody says, “I’m chilling in bed”?‑‑‑Yes, to a degree.

To a degree. Calling somebody a “cheeky monkey”, you would accept that that has – the word “cheeky” has connotations that might be sexual? Do you agree?‑‑‑No, not entirely.

You don’t think that calling a 13 year old “cheeky monkey” that you don’t know has sexual connotations?‑‑‑It could be.

The mother’s seeming ambivalence about the interpretation of this communication “to a degree”, “not entirely”, and “could be” is inconsistent with the evidence one would expect from a mother vigilant about the risk of Mr M grooming her children. The Court finds incredulous the mother’s disagreement with the proposition put to her in cross-examination that the only way that the particular message could be read is with a sexual connotation, particularly in the context of the totality of the communications between Mr M and the young person (Transcript 1 September 2025, p. 25 lines 20-38).

The Court was further taken aback by the mother’s adamant disagreement to viewing as sexually suggestive the following exchange, between Mr M and the young person:
I’m off to bed now –

Winky face –

Thinking of you.

Do you accept, ma’am, that he must have been implying to the child that he was thinking about him in bed?‑‑‑No, I don’t agree with that.

Particularly because it has a winky face. You don’t agree with that?‑‑‑No, I don’t agree with that.

Particularly where it says:

Can’t wait to meet you.

You don’t agree that it still has a sexual implicity in it?‑‑‑No, I don’t agree with that.

And particularly following the next message:

And I’m serious. You are so cute.

I think it’s a winky face again. You don’t think that any of those messages have a sexualised connotation behind them?‑‑‑Can you repeat the question?

You don’t think that any of ‑ ‑ ‑?‑‑‑Rephrase the question, sorry.

Yes. Those messages in a combination ‑ ‑ ‑?‑‑‑Mmm.

Is it your evidence that you do not consider that any of those messages have a sexualised meaning behind them?‑‑‑I don’t believe so.

(Transcript 1 September 2025, p. 38 line 40 to p.39 line 25) (Bold emphasis added).
There are other deeply disconcerting examples consistent with the above. For example, the mother disagreed about the sexual connotation cumulatively arising from the messages describing the young person as a “cheeky monkey”, raising the topic of “sucking on lollipops” and asking the young person “are you at home alone” (Transcripts 1 September 2025, p. 29 lines 28-30). From the Court’s perspective this is not the evidence of a mother with the requisite level of vigilance needed in this case.
Perhaps the nadir of the mother’s stark denials occurred when discussing messages sent by Mr M to the young which said “Send me some pics on my number if you like ��”, to which the young person said “like what pics”, and Mr M said “Surprise me �� I leave it to you ��” (Exhibit ICL2). The mother was invited to agree that the message, in the context, was inviting a sexualised content. Her response was: “It could have invited anything” (Transcript 1 September 2025, p. 31 line 35 to p.32 line 19). Again, this is not the response of a protective mother who had attended, and ostensibly learnt from, a Child Grooming and Sexual Abuse course.
There are several other examples from the cross-examination of the mother. To set out the details would merely be repetitive.
The Court also regrettably finds that some of the mother’s evidence can only be interpreted as calculated in the sense of intending to minimise risks that objectively are far greater than she is willing to accept.
The cross-examination of the mother leaves the Court with no confidence about her protective capacity for the children, notwithstanding the Child Grooming and Sexual Abuse training that she has undertaken. By failing to make logical, sensible and sometimes harmless concessions, the mother has demonstrated not just a profound lack of insight and judgement about the risks to the children’s safety, if they have any communication or contact with Mr M, but also raises in the Court’s mind serious issues about the mother’s credibility. Her cross-examination regrettably creates the impression that the mother prioritises her relationship with Mr M over the safety of the children. Further, the mother’s actions speak of someone wholly enraptured by Mr M. Despite knowing the findings of this Court, she admitted that she married Mr M after only three weeks of engagement (Transcript 1 September 2025, p.59 line 9). She seems to have unequivocally accepted the narrative presented by Mr M, making her myopic with respect to the risk he presents.
Apart from as these serious deficits, there is no doubt that the mother is an excellent mother to the children. Regrettably, there is no change to the Court’s previous findings that the mother has blinkers on in relation to Mr M.

Mr M

The evidence relating to Mr M consisted of the following:
His affidavit filed 25 August 2025 (“Mr M’s affidavit”);
The transcript of his cross-examination on 1 September 2025;
Exhibit R9 namely, the New South Wales Police COPS records;
Exhibits ICL1–ICL4 being records of text and Facebook messages between Mr M, and the young person, and the young person’s father; and
Peripheral evidence relating to Mr M found in other affidavits which will be identified, where relevant.
The Court has once again has consciously chosen not to reproduce in these reasons the entirety of the evidence at Exhibits ICL1–ICL4. Only where necessary will these communications be quoted, in whole or in part. This does not diminish, in any manner, the inappropriateness of Mr M’s communication.
Central to this case is whether it is safe for the children to spend time or communicate with Mr M, in respect of whom serious allegations have been made in relation to the grooming of a young person. The risk to the children is that they themselves could be groomed for sexual purposes by Mr M or that, in any case, based on his prior conduct; regardless of any present risk, that he is an inappropriate person for the children to spend time or communicate with. The evidence of both the mother, and Mr M is that he presents no current risk to the safety of the children, and that he is otherwise an appropriate person for the children to spend time and communicate with. The credibility of Mr M is a critical issue in this case.
For the reasons that follow, this Court does not accept his evidence and finds that in the same way as he groomed a young person, he is an unacceptable risk of future harm to the children and that the safety of the children cannot, in any manner, be promoted by him spending time or communicating with them.
The contention that Mr M’s actions in relation to the young person was somehow consistent with, or proportionate to, the young person’s father’s request that he should “keep an eye on him”, is plainly implausible. The highly sexualised language used in the communication with the young person goes well beyond any mandate to “keep an eye on him”.
When the communication is viewed in its entirety, it is a persistent, calculated, strategy of subtle but escalating engagement behaviour consistent with grooming, over an extended period of time using messaging, telephone and video calls and ultimately an offer of cash. Mr M used language that he knew, or ought to have known, to be sexualised. Some of the messages were highly suggestive. All boundaries of appropriateness were violated. Whilst this behaviour never progressed to actual physical contact, there were many implied invitations for it do so. It is chilling to observe the desensitisation of the young person that occurred over a period of months using electronic communication only. For example, the young person resisted the electronic ‘waving’ and messages initiated by Mr M between 1 February 2019 and 10 July 2019. Thereafter, the young person increasingly engaged with the communication. The intensity and inappropriateness of the communication thereafter increased gradually and included video chats, and the sexual allusions increased rapidly after 30 July 2019. A strong inference of intentional communication for the purpose of grooming is irresistible given the evidence before the Court.
The Court is not satisfied that Mr M has disclosed the full extent of his communication with the young person. There were phone calls. There was video communication. He tells the Court nothing about the content of these communications. The Court does not accept that he did not recall the substance of these communications in late 2019, when (for example) at paragraph 46 of his affidavit of he can recall in detail a conversation with the mother in September 2020.
The Court rejects his contention that he believed the young person to be 16 or 17 years old, instead of the 13 years old that he was at the commencement of their communication. This contention was made for the first time in cross-examination (Transcript 1 September 2025, p.72 lines 0–7). It is not found in his affidavit. There is no record of Mr M telling any of his treating professionals or the Single Joint Expert of this belief. He agreed in cross-examination that he made no attempt to determine how old the young person actually was (Transcript 1 September 2025, p.74 lines 0–5). Given the nature of the contended friendship with young person’s father, it is beggars’ belief that he did not ask the father, who had entrusted him with the responsibility of “keeping an eye” on his son, what his son’s age was. The Court finds that Mr M made up the contention that he believed the young person to be 16 or 17 years old.
The Court does not accept his evidence that the “very dangerous game [he played] with [the young person]” was “never for my own sexual fantasies” (Transcript 1 September 2025, p.72 line 45 to p.73 line 5). The language that he used, in a subtle but escalating fashion, and the explicit nature of his communication with the young person, all make it impossible for the Court to accept that the sole motive of Mr M was “trying to help a very distressed and very unhappy friend that I met at the shop” (Transcript 1 September 2025, p.73 lines 40-45). In any reality it is incomprehensible that a “very distressed” father would be comforted by having an adult male groom his son. The contention is implausible and is rejected by the Court.
Mr M accepted in cross-examination that he had only spoken to the father of the young person, on a number of occasions, both in person and through messages. Whilst the messages commenced in August 2019, Mr M deposed that he met the father of the young person in 2015 and developed what he considered to be “a real friendship” through meeting him at the shop from 2016 (Mr M’s affidavit, paragraph 50). The Court notes that based purely on the evidence of Mr M, up until the beginning of 2019 the relationship is more objectively described as shopkeeper and customer. He deposes that “by mid-2019” his relationship with the young person’s father “through his coming into the shop” developed into one where he believed “we were trusting of each other” (Mr M’s affidavit, paragraph 58). This is implausible.
Mr M deposed that:
I cannot recollect in detail the things [the young person’s father] and I had discussed between January and July 2019, but it included his divorce and the problems he believed [the young person] may have been experiencing

(Affidavit of [Mr M], paragraph 80).
Curiously, the only detailed evidence given by Mr M about the young person’s problems is found at paragraph 46 of his affidavit, where he sets out a conversation he had with the mother “on or around September 2020” after she had reviewed the subpoenaed material “...and alerted me to allegations that I had been grooming an underage person...” He deposed as follows:
[the young persons father] asked me to help him out with [the young person]. He was seriously worried about [the young person’s] wellbeing. He escapes home late at night, he was dressing up as a girl, he was getting bullied at school because of it. He knew what my family and I went through with [Mr MM] (my brother) and asked if I could help him out and see what was going on with [the young person]. How could I say no to [the young person’s father]? He was like family to me.

(Affidavit of [Mr M], paragraph 46).
Accordingly, and doing the best the Court can to gain an insight into his motives, Mr M contends that he was merely trying to “help him out and see what was going on with [the young person]” (Affidavit of Mr M, paragraph 46). In the messages between Mr M and the young person’s father, he confirms this in the following terms:
[The young persons father], I want to clear my position with yesterday ok... You had asked me to keep an eye on [the young person] and to see what he gets up to...

(Exhibit ICL1).
Whilst the date of this communication is not clear on the face of the document, the next few messages assist the Court to date this message as late 2019, which is when Mr M contends that his communication with the young persons father ended. In any event, in the messages that follow from the young person’s father make a number of accusations. Firstly “Offering my 13-year-old son $400 to come to your car is a punishable crime” (Exhibit ICL1). Secondly, “I did not know you are into young boys” (Exhibit ICL1). The Court concludes from this that the young person’s father’s perception of the “help” to be provided by Mr M was very different to that envisaged by Mr M. The Court observes that the independent evidence is consistent with the young person’s father’s perception.
There is a message from Mr M in response to the message about the offer of $400 to the young person. It states, “I did not ask your son that (sic) ask him he wanted me to give him $1,000 to buy shoes” (Exhibit ICL1). It is curious, from the Court’s perspective, that such an important assertion is not found in any evidence lead by the mother or Mr M, but in text messages referenced in the report of Dr CC. Further it is apposite to note this evidence was only produced to the Court as a result of the Court communicating with the parties after the evidence and submissions had concluded to point out that the Court suspected it did not have all of the communications between the young person’s father and Mr M.
The documents were duly produced and tendered. But none of the communications tendered to the Court refer to the young person asking for $1,000 to buy shoes. It is likely that the communications with the young person’s father and the young person before the Court and comprising Exhibits ICL1–ICL4 are incomplete. It is likely that the demand, “he wanted me to give him $1000 to buy shoes”, was made in a telephone or video communication. Yet, Mr M claims not to recall the content of these.
The denial to the young person’s father by Mr M about the $400 is inconsistent with Exhibit ICL3 where he states in a message to the young person: “Please msg me? I just want to meet you like you do too babe �� Reward 400 cash”.
The Court observes that the young person’s father stated, “You are the one who contacted me” (Exhibit ICL1). The response directly after from Mr M indicates agreement with this proposition. However, by contrast in his sworn Court material he explicitly stated that “[the young person’s father], asked me to help him out with [the young person]” (Mr M’s affidavit, paragraph 46). It is more likely than not, the Court finds, that the offer to “help him out with [the young person]” was proffered by Mr M, rather than sought by the young person’s father.
Part of Mr M’s case in response to the claims made against him is that his communication with the young person was never disguised in the sense that his identity and contact number was plainly evident at all relevant times. Moreover, he offered to disclose to the young person’s father all of his communications with the young person (Exhibit ICL1). Implicit in this contention is that no abuse of children would be so overt. The Court notes, but places minimal weight, on this contention. It is this Court’s own experience that sometimes those who present the greatest risk to children hide in plain sight. In any event, the Court has serious doubts whether Mr M has in fact shown the Court “everything”.
There are disturbing contrasts between the evidence of Mr M in cross-examination, and that of the mother. For example, Mr M concedes that his communication with the young person was sexual in nature (Transcript 1 September 2025, p. 73 lines 30-36). However, the cross-examination of the mother, discussed above, demonstrates her ambivalence about this. Mr M insisted, however, that none of the sexual communication was for the purpose of his own sexual gratification (Transcript 1 September 2025, p. 73 lines 24-25). The Court doubts this very much. The evidence of his communication with the young person creates an entirely different impression, when viewed in its totality.
Mr M was cross-examined about his offer to the young person of a “reward” of $400. When asked what he expected in return he said, “absolutely nothing...” (Transcript 1 September 2025, p.79 line 34). He had initially described it in terms of a reward for the young person to go and buy things. Within seconds he contended that he did not expect the young person to do anything with the money or in return for it, and then said he wouldn’t have given him the money at all (Transcript 1 September 2025, p.80 lines 0-6). Mr M’s message presupposes a mutual interest from both parties in physically meeting, with a cash reward to induce the young person if they had doubts. Given the overarching contention that Mr M was messaging the young person to help the young person’s father, it is impossible, indeed perplexing, to imagine a reality as Mr M contends in which both parties may have arrived at a set location only for Mr M to do nothing with the young person and to offer him no money. If that was truly the purpose of the proposed meeting, the Court questions what it would have achieved towards Mr M’s contended aim of helping the young person’s father? The Court finds this evidence implausible.
The Court remains deeply concerned about the evidence of Mr M. His own admissions amount to grooming behaviour. There are so many missing pieces in his story, including what happened in video and telephone calls with the young person, which only he could tell the Court about. His silence ought not to be permitted to assist his case. Where he does explain events, his explanations are implausible.

THE FATHER’S EVIDENCE

The father presented as somewhat of an enigma in this case. Whilst he clearly loves the children, there are curious but important inconsistencies in his case.
Whilst his concerns about the safety of the children in the mother’s care arising out of her relationship with Mr M are accepted by the Court, almost in its entirety, there are aspects of his case that continue to raise serious concerns about his parenting capacity, and his credibility.
In cross-examination he openly displayed a lack of knowledge about the medical issues of the children and, at times, appears to obfuscate about this issue, clearly concerned about how the Court might interpret his seeming insouciance about an important issue.
The way he gave evidence about certain issues was almost bizarre. For example, when cross-examined about compliance with the order for him to spend time with the children, and the requirements of the supervised contact service, his responses were not only vague, but he physically presented as confused and disoriented.
The Court finds that he was completely indifferent in relation to the need to comply with orders and the requirements of the supervised contact service. When spending time with the children, he was inattentive to their needs and relied on the presence other family members to manage the children. The presence of the other members of his family was often problematic for the children, but the Court finds that the father simply could not cope with the children in the absence of extended members of his family. In order to justify the presence of family members he adopted a creative, flexible, and totally self-interested approach to the interpretation of the orders.
His failure to financially contribute towards the maintenance of the children is highly problematic. The Court rejects his evidence that he is unable to make a greater financial contribution towards the children’s needs. The father’s evidence was at its nadir when cross-examined about his finances. He was plainly unconvincing on this issue. He has assets and income available to him which remain undisclosed. He appears to have available to him the seemingly unlimited financial resources of his extended family. He is plainly not working to capacity. This significantly influences the Court’s finding that he has the capacity to meet an order for costs in relation to the Independent Children’s Lawyer.
The most significant finding arising from the evidence of the father is his singular failure, indeed seeming indifference, to addressing the Court’s serious concerns about his parenting capacity identified in earlier judgements. Taking his evidence at its highest, and indeed with a large measure of scepticism by the Court, he has, at most, commenced a Positive Parenting Program and completed 11% of the same. The Court draws the inference that the moment the program commenced by the father became arduous, boring, or otherwise of little interest to the father he elected to stop. The father had an opportunity to tell the Court of any extraneous circumstances that prevented his completion of the course. He did not present any such evidence. The father has not only done little, if anything, to improve his parenting capacity for the children, but he also seems indifferent to the need for him to do so.

THE OTHER EVIDENCE

As the Court has made adverse findings against the mother and Mr M, the two main witnesses in this case on the issue of the safety of the children, the other evidence led in this case is less significant. For example, from what the Court can ascertain from cross-examination alone, the psychological evidence relied on by Mr M from Mr GG and Mr JJ is unreliable for multiple reasons including their reliance solely on the histories given to them by Mr M, and the mother. The Court has no reason to doubt the conclusion of Dr CC that such treaters also administered inappropriate psychological testing. Moreover, those treaters did not have the benefit the Court has of seeing and hearing the entirety of the evidence including cross-examination.
The evidence of Dr CC (except as otherwise stated) carries no weight given the Court’s findings that Mr M had in fact groomed a young person and was in fact a risk to the safety of the children. The recommendations contained in the Single Joint Expert Report cannot be accepted by the Court due to its findings about Mr M, and the mother.

ORDERS IN THE BEST INTERESTS OF THE CHILDREN

This assessment of the best interests of the children is framed by the proposals of the parties. Of course, the Court is not limited to these proposals.
None of the orders proposed would create arrangements that promote the safety of the children, and in the case of the mother’s proposal, specifically safety from the risk of abuse from Mr M. The Court finds that it is more likely than not that Mr M did in fact groom a young person, evidenced by the communication exchanges found in Exhibits ICL1–ICL4. The Court finds that the risk of future abuse by way of grooming the children is an unacceptable one. The Court rejects any alternate explanation proffered by, or on behalf of, Mr M. No evidence led by him, or on his behalf, provides reassurance to the Court that the behaviour that creates the risk to the children’s safety has been adequately acknowledged or addressed, other than in superficial terms.
The risk cannot be mitigated in circumstances where Mr M demonstrates such limited insight into the potential impact of his behaviour on the mother’s children. This limited insight is also manifested by the mother herself. She has not removed the blinkers that seem to hinder her ability to appreciate the risk to her children’s safety presented by the man who is now her husband. The Court acknowledges that the mother’s proposed orders are very carefully crafted with consideration not just to the risk issues for the children but to avoiding sudden changes in their lives, which is a highly relevant consideration given their vulnerabilities. However, even her very carefully crafted orders miss two relevant points. Firstly, the risk of grooming is such an insidious and subtle one, which none of her proposals adequately address. Secondly, the Court is not satisfied that either the mother or Mr M accept that the risk is real. The proposal, as detailed as it is, presents as an attempt to placate the Court.
The orders proposed by the father also, to some extent, raise safety issues. The evidence is replete with instances of the father being unable to cope with the children, even when supported by members of his family. His failure to address, indeed seeming lack of interest in even attempting to address, the deficits in his parenting capacity identified in previous judgements of this Court, create future risks for these children if they time with him were to be extended, as he contends.
Such is the concern about the children’s safety, that any views expressed by the children are not determinative in this case.
The children are developmentally vulnerable. The mother’s evidence is that X has language disorder, low average cognitive skills, Attention Deficit Hyperactivity Disorder (“ADHD”) and anxiety. Her evidence is that Y has ASD level 2, global development delay, ADHD and behavioural difficulties. The mother’s parenting of the children despite these vulnerabilities has been exceptional. But the Court’s concern is that these vulnerabilities make them more susceptible to the risk of grooming by Mr M.
The children are also psychologically and emotionally vulnerable in the sense that the mother seems unable to perceive the risk to her children presented by Mr M. In all other respects she is an excellent mother who can meet all of the children’s needs, and more than adequately has the capacity to parent them.
The father lacks the parenting capacity to spend more time with the children than he already does. The Court carefully considered the possibility of extending the time that he spends with the children but ultimately decided against it. His unwillingness to meet his financial obligations towards the children, his seeming lack of interest in their lives beyond the time that he spends with them, and his failure to address his own issues all contraindicate long-term benefit to the children of more time with him. The children will continue to benefit from knowing the father and enjoying regular and consistent time with him. They will enjoy the benefit of exposure to the paternal extended family. He will always be a significant person to them. The Court does not regard Mr M as a person significant in the lives of the children. They have not seen him for years. Any memory of him is kept alive by the mother. There is no benefit to the children of having a relationship with him as it is not safe to do so.
This is a tragic case. The mother has fallen in love with, and then married, the man who she knew had, at the very least, engaged in highly inappropriate behaviour with a young person. In reality he groomed that young person, a matter of which the mother must reasonably have had some appreciation after she completed the Child Grooming and Sexual Abuse training. Her emotional attachment to Mr M, and in reality, practical and financial reliance on him, has hindered her ability to appreciate what the Court considers to be the objective risks he presents to her children. Nonetheless, the children are safe with her provided they have absolutely no contact or communication with Mr M.
The existing orders for the children to spend time with the father should continue. The father is ordered to pay half the costs that the Independent Children’s Lawyer has sought, in the sum of $15,325.51. The Court does not accept the father’s contention is that he is unable to pay and, in any event, the Court reiterates that “it is well settled that impecuniosity is no absolute barrier to an order for costs” (Jabbar & Gade [2017] FamCAFC 106 at [90]).

COSTS

The Court notes that Order 3 of my orders made 4 April 2025 “reserved and remitted for consideration” the costs of and incidental to the interim proceedings. Should either parent wish to bring an application for costs in relation to any aspect of the proceedings, they are to do so within 28-days of the handing down of these reasons for judgment in accordance with r 12.13 of the Rules. Should any application be filed for costs, directions for the filing of written submissions will issue thereafter.
